exemplar.title: Ib Sl Computer Science Internal Assessment

detailedExemplarData.title: Ib Sl Computer Science Internal Assessment

strengths: Thorough scenario and client investigation with three detailed interview transcripts demonstrating sustained consultation
Comprehensive set of success criteria covering functionality, usability and data handling
weaknesses: Rationale only partially explained; lacks systematic comparison with alternative technologies and mapping to client needs
Missing a formal needs-analysis table to structure client requirements and constraints
Some success criteria could be more quantitative or explicitly tested (e.g., weekly calorie tally, colour-change feedback)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments indicate very strong scenario investigation (band 5/6) and success criteria (band 5/6) but a weaker, partially explained rationale (band 3/6). Holistically this places planning in the 3â4 mark range. A score of 4/6 reflects these high-quality elements tempered by the less systematic rationale.

strengths: Effective use of JavaFX, Scene Builder, SQLite, DAO pattern and logging utilities
Moderate complexity and ingenuity demonstrated (MVC architecture, SHA-256 hashing, dynamic UI updates, CRUD operations)
All external sources properly cited with explanations of key techniques in commented code snippets
weaknesses: Database design lacks rationale for normalization and clear foreign-key annotations
UML and flowcharts miss detailed notation (multiplicity, start/end nodes, labelled decision points)
Several code snippets contain typos and unclear variable names, affecting readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Across complexity (band 6/12), use of tools (band 6/12) and documentation (band 5/12), the studentâs work sits solidly in the 5â8 band. An overall Development score of 6/12 reflects this strong but not fully comprehensive execution.

strengths: Detailed and complete record of tasks spanning the entire project with clear actions, outcomes and dates
Comprehensive design overview including screenshots, UML diagram, hierarchical chart and multiple flowcharts
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in the task log (one missing time estimate)
Design overview lacks discussion of internal data structures, class interactions and algorithmic choices
Test plan is only a basic outline and lacks identifiers and dates for traceability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Record of tasks is strong (band 5/6), design overview sits at the top of band 3â4 (band 4/6), and the test plan is basic (band 3/6). Averaging these performances yields a solid 4/6 for Solution Overview.

strengths: Core functionality works well (login, schedule navigation, exercise CRUD, calorie counter, colour-change feedback)
Underlying DAO/MVC structure supports future extensibility
weaknesses: Some client-requested features (e.g., favourite routines) were not implemented
Hard-coded UI and database logic make extension more difficult than ideal
No video demonstration provided to fully verify functionality
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Product functionality aligns with the top band (3/4) but with minor omissions; extensibility is possible but requires noticeable refactoring (band 2/4). Holistically this criterion merits 3/4.

strengths: Evaluation table clearly maps success criteria to evidence of achievement
Well-documented client feedback through a structured post-development interview
Recommendations are realistic and based on user comments
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks critical reflection on limitations, test results and challenges
Recommendations are not prioritised or linked back to specific test outcomes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides a partial evaluation against success criteria (band 3/6), some detailed client feedback (band 4/6), and largely realistic recommendations (band 3/6). Holistically this supports a mid-band score of 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Screenshots, annotated descriptions of every window, UML, hierarchical chart and multiple flowcharts give a **basic-to-good overview** of structure and navigation.  However, there is limited discussion of data structures, class interactions or algorithm choices; therefore the work sits at the top of the 3â4 band (score 4) rather than the fully comprehensive 5â6 band.

The rationale identifies JavaFX because of cross-platform compatibility and the authorâs existing skills, and briefly contrasts the proposed app with existing fitness apps that lack combined scheduling and exercise editing.  While these points give a **partial explanation**, the argument is not systematically justified against client requirements or alternative technologies, so it fits the âpartially explainedâ descriptor (3â4 band) at the lower end.

The student makes **appropriate and effective use** of JavaFX, Scene Builder, SQLite, DAO and logging utilities.  Screenshots show controllers interacting with the database, error alerts, and styling.  While there is no evidence of advanced tools such as CI/CD, the use of existing libraries is clearly beyond âlimitedâ, placing it solidly in the 5-8 band (score 6).

The table covers key functions (sign-up, sign-in, add/remove exercise, calorie goal) and mentions normal/abnormal data, expected outcomes and error handling.  Coverage is **basic**; some criteria (colour-change, weekly tally, security) are missing and test cases are not numbered or dated.  This matches a âbasic outlineâ (band 3â4) at the lower end, score 3.

Four recommendations (password reset, weekly calories, save routines, filter exercises) are realistic and linked to user comments but lack technical detail or prioritisation.  This is âlargely realisticâ rather than âwell-justifiedâ, so mid-band 3.

All external sources (Oracle docs, OpenJFX, Scene Builder, SQLite) are cited, and key techniques (database connection, hashing, DAO) are explained with commented code snippets.  Some snippets lack full citation or reflection, so the explanation is not fully âcomprehensiveâ.  Lower mid-band 5 is appropriate.

The record of tasks spans the whole project with 15 numbered tasks, each showing action, outcome, time estimate and target date.  Links to criteria letters are included.  Minor inconsistencies (e.g. missing time for one sub-task) prevent perfection, but the log clearly demonstrates development progress, thus high in the 5-6 band (score 5).

The student implements a multi-window JavaFX application with DAO pattern, password hashing, dynamic colour updates, calorie calculations and CRUD operations on SQLite.  These features show **moderate complexity and some ingenuity** (e.g. SHA-256 hashing, observable lists, MVC-style Model).  Algorithms are straightforward, so it does not reach the highest band, but comfortably merits mid-band score 6.

Twelve success criteria are listed, each observable and measurable (e.g. log-in, edit exercise, colour change when complete, calorie deficit calculation).  They cover functionality, usability and data handling, providing a **comprehensive range**.  A few criteria could be more quantitative, so score 5 rather than 6.

The appendix records a structured post-development interview in which the client comments on each feature and suggests improvements.  This is **some, well-documented feedback**, though not triangulated with adviser feedback; top of 3â4 band (score 4).

DAO structure and MVC separation mean new windows or database columns could be added, but hard-coded column names and coupled UI logic would still require noticeable refactoring.  Hence expansion is possible but **difficult**, aligning with mid band 1â2; award 2.

The student names a real client, explains the fitness problem in depth and embeds **three separated interview transcripts** that capture needs, constraints and desired features.  The dialogue shows dates and follow-up questions, demonstrating sustained, detailed consultation rather than a single contact.  This aligns with the descriptor of âthoroughly describedâ evidence, although a more formal needs-analysis table could strengthen it, hence mid-band 5 not 6.

Narrative, screenshots and the client interview indicate that the core features work: log-in, schedule navigation, exercise CRUD, calorie counter and colour change.  No video is supplied here, but written evidence implies the product **functions well**.  Minor issues (e.g. favourite routines not implemented) keep it to the bottom of the top band (score 3 of 4).

The student checks every success criterion in a table and summarises evidence, but provides little critical reflection (e.g. no reference to test results or limitations).  This fits a **partial evaluation**âmore than âlimitedâ, less than âfullâ.

annotations.comment: The scenario description is clear, but it would benefit from a formal needs-analysis table to systematically capture client requirements and constraints.
The currency symbol is rendered as â\epsilonâ instead of ââ¬â which may confuse readersâcorrect the symbol for clarity.
The rationale names JavaFX familiarity and cross-platform support, but should systematically compare alternatives and map chosen features to client needs.
The success-criteria section lists many functions but could include quantitative targets (e.g., max response time, error rates) to make evaluation more measurable.
Although comprehensive, the criteria omit explicit tests for color-change feedback and weekly calorie tallyâconsider adding these for full coverage.
The task log is impressively detailed with actions, outcomes and dates demonstrating strong planning discipline.
In the ROT table some entries omit consistent time estimates for subtasksâensure every task row includes a clear duration.
Stray mathematical symbols (e.g. â\Boxâ) in the login window list distract from clarity; replace or remove them.
The login form mock-up lacks detail on input length constraints and validation feedback; specify expected field behavior.
The sign-up window description omits how duplicate usernames are handled and what error feedback appears.
Schedule window list lacks mention of how the app responds to server or data-load failuresâconsider what the user sees on error.
In the workout routine window mock-up, clarify behavior when no exercises are present (empty state).
The âAdd new exerciseâ form does not specify validation rules for numeric inputs (frequency/calories); include required formats.
Calorie counter list omits how non-numeric input is handled other than a generic alertâdetail specific validation (e.g. regex).
Database structure notes fields but omits rationale for normalization and indexing strategyâadd a brief explanation.
The Users table lacks explicit foreign-key annotations linking exercises to usersâdenote relationships clearly.
UML diagram shows classes but omits multiplicity notation on associations; include cardinalities for clarity.
The login/sign-up flowchart lacks explicit start/end nodes and directional arrows; add these for unambiguous control flow.
The decision points in the Add Exercise flowchart are unlabeled; present clear conditions on diamond shapes.
Test plan table is detailed but lacks test case identifiers and dates for traceabilityânumber and date each case.
The SQLite connection code contains typos (âfthis.connâ, âmsg:â) indicating OCR or copy errorsâcorrect method signatures for compilation.
Color-change code snippet has garbled variable names (e.g. âÃ¼Ã¼eâ, âutleâ) which obscure logicâclean up for readability.
The folder structure diagram is presented as inline text, making it hard to parseârepresent packages visually or as a proper list.
Bibliography entry for JavaFX lacks an access date; include retrieval dates for web sources.
The evaluation table clearly maps criteria to outcomes, but no critical reflection on limitations or challenges is provided.
The evaluation table demonstrates thorough mapping of success criteria to functionalityâthis provides clear evidence of meeting objectives.
Recommendations are realistic but lack prioritization; consider ranking them based on impact and effort.
The recommendations section describes ideas but doesnât link them back to test results or success criteria; strengthen justification by relating to specific findings.
